# Failed Trials: Timing is Everything for Stripe Payments

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

 

 

 

Philip Pages 

 2w Edited 

 

- Report this post 
- 

 

"They're gaming my trial," the CEO told me. 51,000 ratings. $22M ARR. He was wrong. He was beyond frustrated, convinced he had a "freeloader" problem. I've audited over 100+ Stripe accounts and have seen this pattern before. So I pulled his Stripe data and picked one failed trial at random. The data told a different story: 1/ User signs up and uses the app for 7 straight days. 2/ Day 8: Stripe attempts to charge $14.99. 3/ Result: Declined for insufficient funds. Stripe retried on Tuesday. It failed. Stripe retried on Thursday. It failed again. After four attempts, the subscription was canceled. "This user was active every single day of their trial," I said. "Does that look like someone trying to game the system?" He went quiet. That card would have cleared on Friday. Why? Because Friday was payday. The customer had the money 72 hours later, but Stripe had already cancelled the subscription. We started retrying at better times. Fridays. The first of the month. 9:00 AM local time when paychecks hit. The result was +$79k in new MRR within a month from users they had already given up on. TAKEAWAY The difference between a "failed trial" and $79k in new MRR is often just 72 hours. Stop treating your fans like bad debt. If you are retrying on a Tuesday, you are leaving your best revenue on the table. Fix your timing. The money is already there.

Stripe says the payment succeeded. Your app says it didn’t. That gap is where a lot of SaaS products quietly lose revenue. Most webhook systems work fine early on until scale introduces timeouts, duplicate events, and failed upgrades. The fix usually isn’t Stripe. It’s architecture. Fast acknowledgements. Async queues. Idempotency. The backend patterns separating stable SaaS products from fragile ones are changing fast. 🐝#BeeTechy#SoftwareArchitecture#Stripe#SaaS#BackendDevelopment#CloudEngineering

Bee Techy, LLC. 1w

Webhook failures usually don’t crash loudly. They quietly create refunds, support tickets, failed upgrades, and revenue leakage.

Reply 1 Reaction 2 Reactions

To view or add a comment, sign in

1w

Three Stripe webhook failures I keep seeing in production: 1. Treating the webhook handler as the source of truth for fulfilment, with no idempotency check. First retry creates a duplicate order. Second retry creates another. Customer sees one charge, three orders. 2. Returning a 200 before the database write completes. Webhook says delivered, your DB says nothing happened. The reconciliation job at 3am tells you the bad news. 3. No signature verification. Anyone who finds the endpoint can fire fake events and you'll process them. None of these are clever bugs. They're shortcuts that production exposes the second traffic gets real. Stripe's docs cover all three. People skip them because the happy path works in dev. What's the worst webhook bug you've shipped? (No judgement, we've all done it.)#StripePayments#WebDevelopment#NextJS#SaaS#FullStackDevelopment

# Reconciling Multi-Processor Payment Reliability Models

Founding Engineer · AI / B2B SaaS · Ships in days · Live: invobi.app · arabastack.com · 5daysites.co.uk · MSc Hertfordshire

2w

Multi-processor support doesn't mean abstracting API calls. It means reconciling three different reliability models pretending to be payment systems. Stripe retries failed webhooks 3 times over a few hours. Paystack retries up to 72 hours. Flutterwave sits between them. Each one has its own signature scheme, replay window, and idempotency contract. When you build a multi-processor payment layer, the integrations are the easy part. What's actually hard is getting those three reliability models to compose — so downstream side-effects (mark invoice paid, send receipt, fire analytics, update revenue) don't fire twice on duplicate webhooks. Most multi-processor bugs hide a layer above the webhook handler. They live in the assumption that "event acknowledged" and "side-effects safely completed" are the same thing. They're not. Once retries and ordering enter, you're looking at two different guarantees. ARABA Stack ships with that reconciliation already wired: • Idempotency keys on the side-effect tier, not just on database writes • `email_sent_at` guards on transactional sends • Per-processor retry semantics built into the router • Stateless edge functions for the webhook handlers themselves Every other boilerplate's README skips this part. The first time my code charged a customer twice, I learned what idempotency really means. A user clicked "Pay" → the request timed out → the client retried. The transaction went through twice. Customer support flooded. Finance reconciled for hours. I had thought I understood idempotency — turns out I didn't. What I thought it was: - Using PUT instead of POST - Trusting the client to send the same payload - Catching the duplicate at the database What it actually is: designing the API so the same request always returns the same result, no matter how many

Customer Centricity: Turning Chargeback Management into a Competitive Advantage

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

Customer centricity is often described as a mindset, but in practice it is a system. It is the structure behind how a business listens, responds, and improves. This structure is tested most clearly in moments where expectations break. Chargebacks are one of those moments. They begin as customer frustration, long before a dispute is filed. Most of that frustration shows up in small signals that often go unnoticed because they live in different systems, different teams, or different parts of the customer journey. By the time the dispute reaches the merchant, revenue is already at risk and the customer relationship has weakened.

Treating chargebacks only as a financial event misses their strategic value. Every dispute contains insight into how a customer interpreted an experience. When merchants learn to read those signals early, they begin to create a more predictable, transparent, and trustworthy journeys. This shift requires more than traditional case-by-case management. It calls for a more integrated, intelligence-led and customer-centric approach.

Why a More Intelligent Approach is Now Essential

The volume and complexity of disputes have changed significantly. Subscription renewals, on-demand services, and travel disruptions create environments where confusion and timing issues are common. Friendly fraud continues to rise as customers treat chargebacks as a convenient resolution path, and recent findings from LexisNexis Risk Solutions indicate that first-party misuse now accounts for 36 percent of all reported fraud, a sharp increase compared to previous years. Regulations continue to evolve, and issuers rely more heavily on automated decision systems. These shifts make it critical for merchants to detect patterns early and take informed action before customer issues escalate.

Many teams attempt this manually, but manual monitoring cannot keep pace with the scale or nuance of modern dispute behavior. Insights become reactive rather than proactive. Decisions rely on limited context. Opportunities to prevent disputes are missed.

This is where the right chargeback solutions partner can make a meaningful difference. At Chargeback Gurus (CBG), we have seen how much clarity merchants gain when disputes are no longer viewed as isolated eve

Keiboarder

1mo

Prepaid users waited over a year for a platform that couldn’t keep its promises. In this project, the founder owned the customer risk, but the absence of technical leadership left the team exposed to launch delays, unstable calculations, and a revolving door of engineering firms. If you try to patch over missing technical leadership with more contractors, you just multiply the handoffs and the headaches. Every missed release compounds user frustration and erodes trust. We have seen this before. When the product space is drapery workroom pricing and project management, and the blocker is launch delays with no test coverage, the business risk is simple: users leave before you ever get a chance to serve them. We do not wait for the next outage to act. Technical debt is not a rounding error. Ownership is not optional, even if your background is textiles, not tech. The longer you wait to stabilize the platform, the more expensive every fix becomes. Investors notice. So do your customers. Moving Beyond SAQ A: Using Real‑World Threat Vectors to Select Meaningful PCI DSS Requirements for iFrame‑based eCommerce Service Providers

Hosted iFrame TPSP

## Introduction

A lot of security impacting service providers are stuck in an awkward gap.

They are not merchants, so they cannot claim SAQ A eligibility, but they also never see a primary account number in the clear. When PCI DSS 4.0 and the new web skimming controls arrive, those providers suddenly find themselves asked to “show applicability” for a standard that was clearly written with merchants in mind.

If you are providing iFrame based eCommerce services, this probably sounds familiar.

This article offers a practical way out of that gap. It combines the MITRE ATT&CK matrix with the PCI SSC’s own FAQ guidance to justify a defensible, risk based selection of PCI DSS requirements for your Report on Compliance.

---

## Why SAQ A is the wrong tool for your job?

PCI SSC’s FAQ on SAQ eligibility is very clear. The criteria for each Self Assessment Questionnaire exist to make sure that only very specific environments use that cut‑down requirement set.

The FAQ also explains that SAQ eligibility cannot be used as a shortcut to decide applicability of PCI DSS requirements for a full ROC. In other words:

- If you are a service provider, SAQ A does not apply to you at all. Your default is SAQ D for service providers or a full ROC.
- If you are a merchant and your compliance accepting entity tells you to complete a ROC, the SAQ A requirement list is not a master checklist for “what applies”

That makes sense once you remember why SAQ A exists. It assumes a pure redirect or iFrame model where the merchant’s own website has no ability to affect the security of the payment page. In reality, most hosted payment solutions, tag managers and platform integrations are much messier.

If you are the one building and operating those iFrames and supporting JavaScript, you are already outside the SAQ A world. You are a security impacting service provider.

---

## What PCI SSC expects from security impacting service providers?

FAQ 1580 answers the key question for you. It says PCI DSS is intended not only for entities that store, process or transmit cardholder data, but also for service providers that could impact the security of payment account data.

It gives three examples of how that can happen:

- Providing services that directly or indirectly facilitate the storage, processing or transmission of payment

### What is PCI DSS, and Why Does it Matter?

PCI DSS is a set of security standards designed to ensure that all entities accepting, processing, storing, or transmitting credit card data maintain secure environments. ### 3. Implement Security Controls with Cyber Hygiene in Mind

Maintaining strong cyber hygiene is critical for PCI DSS compliance. Key measures include:

- Compliance Automation Tools: Leverage solutions like Drata to continuously monitor compliance posture and ensure all controls are in place.
- Remote Access Protection: Use VPN services to secure connections when accessing systems remotely.
- Data Encryption: Ensure that sensitive payment data is encrypted during transmission and storage.
- Network Security: Use firewalls to secure payment processing environments.

PCI DSS Audit Checklist for Developers

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

PCI Audit Checklist

When it comes to PCI DSS audits, developers aren’t just part of the process—they’re at the heart of it. From code to infrastructure, everything you build shapes your company’s ability to pass a PCI audit. Whether you're in a SaaS startup, a payment gateway, or an eCommerce platform, your dev decisions directly impact compliance.

This guide lays out a developer-specific PCI DSS audit checklist to help you ship secure code, harden your stack, and get audit-ready—fast.

🔍 Why Developers Matter in PCI DSS

PCI DSS (Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard) isn’t just for compliance officers or QSAs. Developers are responsible for:

- Building and maintaining secure systems that store, process, or transmit cardholder data
- Ensuring secure coding practices are followed across the SDLC
- Configuring access, logging, encryption, and segmentation
- Automating scans, updates, and secure deployments

So when the audit comes knocking, your preparedness determines the outcome.

✅ PCI DSS Developer Audit Checklist

1. 🔐 Know Your Scope

Key Actions:

- Identify all components that handle cardholder data (CHD) and sensitive authentication data (SAD)
- Document APIs, databases, microservices, and third-party integrations
- Classify zones: cardholder data environment (CDE) vs non-CDE

Pro Tip: Reduce scope early by segmenting systems and avoiding CHD storage where not necessary.

2. 🧱 Secure Coding Practices (Req. 6.5)

Key Actions:

- Follow OWASP Top 10 guidelines
- Use input validation, output encoding, and error handling
- Avoid hardcoded credentials or secrets in code
- Use dependency scanning tools (like Snyk, OWASP Dependency-Check)

Bonus Tip: Implement code review checklists with security as a mandatory step.

3. 🔒 Authentication & Access Controls (Req. 7 & 8)

Key Actions:

- Enforce MFA for all admin and dev access
- Use role-based access control (RBAC)
- Ensure no shared accounts or default passwords
- Automate session timeout and lockout policies

4. 📜 Logging & Monitoring (Req. 10)

Key Actions:

- Log access, changes, and authentication attempts for all systems handling CHD
- Store logs centrally (e.g., SIEM, ELK)
- Keep logs tamper-proof and retain for at least 1 year

Developer Task: Ensure apps and APIs are logging securely without storing CHD.

5
title: PCI DSS Audit Checklist for Developers

---

A forensic exposé went viral this week. A well-funded, YC-backed compliance platform was caught generating identical audit reports for hundreds of clients. Same auditor conclusions. Same test procedures. Same typos — across 259 SOC 2 Type II reports. Pre-written verdicts before any auditor reviewed a single piece of evidence. Fabricated board meeting minutes. Trust pages that declared security measures that were never implemented.

The auditors? Indian certification mills operating through empty US shell entities, rubber-stamping reports they didn't write.

The clients? Hundreds of companies — including firms handling PHI for millions of US citizens — walking around with compliance certificates that won't survive their next enterprise security review.

If you work in security or compliance, you probably already know which company I'm talking about. But this article isn't about them. It's about the model they represent — and why it was always going to end here.

---

### The "Compliant in Days" Lie

The pitch is seductive: AI-native compliance. Get your SOC 2 in days, not months. Agentic automation that eliminates the busywork.

Here's what that actually means in practice: pre-populated templates you click "accept" on. Policies that claim you have an MDM when your process is a screenshot of Mac firewall settings. Risk assessments that are identical across every customer. Evidence that exists before you've done any work.

The speed isn't coming from better technology. It's coming from skipping the work.

And for a while, it doesn't matter. Many enterprises tick a box when they see a SOC 2 report and move on. The certificate opens doors. Revenue comes in. Everyone's happy.

Until someone actually reads the report.

---

### Where It Breaks

The real cost of compliance theatre doesn't show up as a line item. It shows up as:

I Spent 12 Years Building Rural Fintech. Here’s What Actually Matters.

### Why technology alone failed - and what finally worked in community finance

Part 1 of a LinkedIn Series on Community Finance

Four years ago, on a sunny afternoon, seated at my desk at our Kiwatule office in Kampala, I spent nearly 6 hours doing almost nothing - just staring into space.

This routine repeated itself for three consecutive weeks.

It wasn’t burnout. It wasn’t laziness.

I was exhausted from simply building - hoping that if we kept iterating long enough, we would eventually crack the puzzle of how to create a product customers truly loved and were willing to pay for.

Eventually, I was forced to confront a question I could no longer avoid:

Was Ensibuuko truly changing lives - or were we just building something that was merely good to have?

### When Infrastructure Isn’t Enough

For nearly eight years, we had been building a SaaS banking platform to automate the operations of community banking schemes like SACCOs and savings groups. Like many fintech founders, we believed - deeply - that fixing banking infrastructure would fix access to finance in rural communities.

Digitize records. Automate processes. Replace paper with software.

It sounded logical. It felt inevitable.

But we were wrong.

Those years were long. Frustrating. Painful.

Because while infrastructure matters, infrastructure alone does not unlock rural finance.

### The Hard Truth About Financial Inclusion

Yes, paper-based records and cash-heavy operations keep formal financial services out of rural communities. Anyone who has worked closely with SACCOs and savings groups understands this reality.

But here is the uncomfortable truth:

What is infrastructure without capital?

Access to finance, in rural, fails not just because systems are broken, but because both sides of the credit equation are broken.

On the supply side, financial institutions struggle with risk, cost, data gaps, and trust. On the demand side, rural communities operate in volatile, informal environments that mainstream finance was never designed for.

Rural finance is far more complicated than the popular metaphor of “build the road.”

You must also bring the cars. And fuel the cars. And make sure the drivers know where they are going.

### The Decision That Changed Everything

In 2022, we made a hard decision - one that fundamentally reshaped our company.

We would stop building just technology.

Bam Azizi, CEO of Mesh Connect - From Credit Card Debt to Raising $130M, Building the Rails for a $37T Market, Executing a Wild Pivot

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

This article is part of Fintech Leaders, a newsletter with 75,000+ builders, entrepreneurs, investors, regulators, and students of financial services. I invite you to share and sign up. If you enjoy this conversation, please consider leaving a review on Apple, Spotify, or Youtube.

---

In this episode, I interview Bam Azizi, Co-Founder and CEO of Mesh Connect, a fintech infrastructure company that has transformed from a consumer-focus to a fast-growing B2B platform serving hundreds of corporate clients including Revolut, and Coinbase. After raising nearly $130 million, including a Series B closed in just one month, Mesh is now focusing on serving the stablecoin ecosystem.

## In this episode, we discuss:

### The rollercoaster journey from B2C to B2B and the painful but necessary pivot decisions

> "It was one of the saddest days for me that we had to let half of the team go... but at some point, you have to make sure that the ship doesn't sink, right?"

Building for the wrong customer forced a painful but necessary pivot. Bam Azizi's story with Mesh follows a familiar but painful pattern of several tech and fintech companies. Starting with consumer ambitions only to discover the real opportunity was in B2B infrastructure. Initially called "Front App," Mesh began as a consumer platform allowing users to connect various financial accounts like Robinhood and Coinbase in one place.

Despite attracting billions of dollars in connected assets, monetization proved elusive and the company found itself desperately seeking for revenue. It was at this point that they made the difficult decision to productize their infrastructure and eventually let go of nearly half the team—primarily those from B2C backgrounds who couldn't transition to B2B skills.

They launched their connectivity API on Product Hunt to see if anyone would bite and the response was immediate: FIVE enterprise customers on day one. Three months later, they sunset the consumer app entirely, focusing exclusively on B2B. The pivot was both brutal and brilliant—a textbook example of recognizing when you're "barking up the wrong tree" and making the hard decisions for survival.

### Why stablecoins represent a $37 trillion opportunity

I Built the Right Product and Still Shut It Down: Winding Down Fimio, Inc

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

After 4 years of building, I've made the decision to wind down Fimio, Inc., the AI infrastructure company I founded in 2022.

First, I want to thank my investors for believing in me and backing this journey. I'm grateful for your trust, and I'm proud that we were able to return remaining capital rather than burn through it.

When I left GitHub, I wasn't sure what was next. In the language of Jim Collins in "What to Make of a Life," I was facing a cliff. I had been pushing hard for 16 years straight, driven by an inner fire to survive and build something of myself. Instead of sitting in the fog, I plowed headlong into what I thought was next: founding a venture-backed startup.

I love building. And through building Fimio, I learned a few things about myself that I hadn't fully recognized before:

1. I really REALLY believe in permissionless systems.
2. I believe die-hard in open source.
3. I love builders.

I started Fimio to focus on web3 security, then took my infrastructure learnings and pivoted post-SBF into AI infrastructure (sandboxed inference deployments). What I was building turned out to be exactly what the world needs now.

So I should have been thrilled. But I wasn't.

The building and shipping part lit me up. The selling did not. I had designed something beautifully suited for enterprises, which meant founder-led enterprise sales. And that work (spending most of my time with the buyer not the user, the long cycles, the focus on closing a deal instead of shipping a product) ran against everything that energizes me. I fell into the classic trap of the highly technical founder: build something great, then discover the go-to-market motion requires becoming someone you're not.

Instead of forcing it, I chose to be honest with myself. I shut it down, returned cash to my investors, and stepped back to learn more about who I am and how I'm wired; so that next time, I can build something where the entire journey, not just the product, aligns with my strengths.

To every founder wrestling with a similar tension: it's not failure to recognize misalignment. It's clarity. And clarity is the most valuable thing you can carry into whatever comes next.

Onward.

Today, it’s easy to take for granted the ease with which we can perform all kinds of financial transactions on our smartphones. Several years ago, this was not the case. Plaid played a key role in shaping this digital financial transformation. 

 

In this episode of Building One, we explore Plaid with Zachary Perret , its CEO and co-founder. Zach started building fintech as a personal passion. In the decade since its founding, Plaid has tremendously worked to modernize the financial industry and reduce the friction within it. 

 

Given how transformational Plaid has been as a B2B product, it’s amazing that the company started with a totally different B2C model that failed. Zach is very candid about early missteps and crucial lessons he’s still learning amid Plaid’s great success. One of the things I was most struck by in our conversation is how Zach’s inquisitive nature naturally dovetails with a learning mindset. 

 

 

Here are some of my favorite takeaways from our conversation: 

 

### Knowing where to find empathy 

 

> “The reason that we failed on the consumer side is because we didn't have the right level of customer empathy, and the reason that we were able to be successful at the beginning with Plaid is because we had a high degree of empathy with our B2B customers, with our developer customers.” 

 

The best source of empathy in building products is self-empathy. Empathy can be a bit of an overused term, but it all comes down to knowing and even feeling the experience of others…or yourself. I sat down with Ali Mazanderani, Chairman of Lesaka, a nasdaq traded, leading fintech in Southern Africa with over $400 million in revenue and 2 million active customers. Ali is a fintech veteran investor and builder, who’s been closely involved with several multi-billion dollar fintechs in emerging markets, including Stone, Pine Labs, Kushki, Thunes and is also a co-founder of Teya.

We discussed why Southern Africa is one of the most exciting fintech opportunities in the world, how Lesaka is turning 120,000 merchants from cash-only to digital, the strategy behind acquiring Bank Zero, and why being a publicly traded company is a feature, not a bug.

## Why Africa’s Biggest Fintech Opportunity Is Just Getting Started

Ali Mazanderani has spent two decades investing in fintech revolutions across emerging markets, from Brazil to India to the Middle East. He backed StoneCo before it was a household name, sat on the board of Network International, and led an investment into Pine Labs. When he decided to stop investing and start building, he chose Southern Africa, a market most global investors have largely ignored.

The conventional assumption is that South Africa, as the continent's most industrialized economy, would be its fintech leader. It’s actually the opposite. To give you an idea, in Brazil and Egypt, the market cap of the four largest fintechs has reached roughly 30% of the market cap of the four largest banks. In South Africa, that figure is just 5%. South African electronic payments have gone from representing essentially zero of all merchant transactions to only about 10%, meaning 90% of South African merchants still do not accept a single digital payment. These figures define Lesaka’s bullish thesis.

According to Ali, three factors explain the lag of fintech adoption in South Africa:

# CPG founder's honest failure: ignoring the numbers

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

1mo

I knew my brand was dead three months before I admitted it. The spreadsheet said it. I just didn't want to read it. Every week I'd open the cash flow model. Every week I'd find a reason to believe next month would be different. "We just need that reorder from Whole Foods." "The new buyer will get it." "Once we hit velocity targets, the math works." The math never worked. Here's what I was actually doing: Paying for inventory I couldn't move. Running promos that bled margin to hit velocity I couldn't sustain. Telling my co-founder "we're close" while watching the runway shrink. The product was good. People who used it LOVED it. But love doesn't pay invoices. What killed my CPG brand L'uvalla wasn't competition. It wasn't the product. It wasn't even bad retail partners. It was me refusing to look at the numbers and make a decision. I kept optimizing a business model that was already broken. The hardest part? I still believed in what we were building. Helping people get healthier skin felt meaningful. It was the most fulfilling work I'd ever done. But fulfillment doesn't fix negative gross margin. If I could go back: 𝟭. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗻𝘂𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗮𝗿𝗲𝗻'𝘁 𝗹𝘆𝗶𝗻𝗴. 𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗮𝗿𝗲. If you have to squint at the spreadsheet to make it work, it doesn't work. 𝟮. 𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝗹𝗼𝘃𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗺𝗶𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗸𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀. Passion without margin is an expensive hobby. 𝟯. 𝗦𝗲𝘁 𝗮 𝗸𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗯𝗲𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱 𝗼𝗻𝗲. Decide in advance: "If X doesn't happen by Y, I'm out." The clarity saves you from yourself. That failure taught me more about CPG than any success ever could. Now at Promomash, I see founders in the same fog I was in. Good products. Bad math. Hope as a strategy. The ones who survive aren't the ones with the best product. They're the ones who read the spreadsheet and make the hard call before the spreadsheet makes it for them. What's the decision you're avoiding right now? ➕ Follow Yuval Selik for CPG truth most founders learn too late.

Daniel Lohman, CPSA 1mo

An example of how manual processes quickly spiral out of control

In my last article, I shared how I moved from client support to leading global compliance in payments businesses and eventually into tech. Not because I wanted a new job title, but because I kept hitting the same wall… over and over. I knew what good KYB should look like, but I could never find a solution that actually delivered it.

This article is about that wall: the KYB vendor landscape. The promises, the gaps, the headaches and why I joined Detected to build what I had been looking for and never found.

---

The Landscape Today: More Logos Than Solutions

The KYB vendor space is crowded. On the surface, it looks like you have got plenty of options with established names, shiny start-ups, tools for every acronym but scratch the surface, and most platforms fall into one of three buckets:

- Single purpose tools that are great at one thing but leave your team managing five systems and praying your colour coded spreadsheet is up to date
- Well known platforms that haven’t kept up with rule engines that break as soon as your policy strays from their default
- Fancy KYC tools rebadged as KYB, where business onboarding is treated like a solo account with extra steps

While they might demo beautifully, they will also break down quickly when the real world shows up with nested structures, conflicting registries, or policies that don’t fit in a dropdown.

---

The Moment You Realise the Tools Won’t Save You

Let me set the scene: I was at Tipalti, running global onboarding and compliance ops. We were growing and growing fast. The kind of fast that makes your team Slack channel a mix of celebration and low-grade panic.

We needed a KYB solution that could scale with us and help us onboard multi-entity businesses across multiple jurisdictions ideally without doubling the size of the team every quarter.

So, I sat through demo after demo. Every vendor had jazzy decks, punchy taglines, and impressive logos but the minute I asked a real-world question like “Can I see how this handles a corporate structure with three layers in the UAE?” or “Can I automate triggers based on business changes?”, I’d get one of two things:

# Innokenty Bodrov’s Post

Product Leader | Fintech & EdTech | AI & Data-Driven Solutions | Platform & CX Expert | 17+ yrs in Tech

1mo

AMLD6 made compliance officers personally criminally liable for AML failures. Then the industry handed the decisions to AI. Nobody has fully worked out what happens next. AMLD6 extended criminal liability to the individuals running compliance — the MLRO, the CCO, the Head of AML. If the program fails badly enough, it's not a fine for the institution. It's a criminal case with a name on it. At the same time, the industry has spent five years automating exactly those decisions. Alert triage. Transaction monitoring. Risk scoring. Document verification. The AI runs the first pass. Humans review what it flags — or don't, if the volume is high enough. If an AI system misses a suspicious activity report — who is criminally liable? The compliance officer who approved the threshold configuration? The vendor who built the model? The risk team that set the parameters? Right now, the answer is legally untested and everyone assumes it's someone else's problem. The counterargument is familiar: humans miss SARs too, and we don't prosecute every failure. True. But a human error is individual. An AI error is systematic — one miscalibrated threshold misses every case that pattern applies to, for as long as it runs. Regulators haven't fully adapted their accountability frameworks to this. What makes it harder is the explainability trade-off. The AI that performs best is often a black box. The AI regulators are comfortable with — auditable, explainable, defensible in a proceeding — tends to be less accurate. Performance or explainability. Most current systems make you choose. At Sumsub, we're working on both simultaneously — and that's not a comfortable position to describe, but it's the honest one. Speed and conversion: checks that take seconds, flows that don't make users feel like suspects. And auditability: every AI-assisted decision needs a paper trail the compliance officer can defend by name. Those goals are genuinely in tension. The product challenge is to move both numbers in the right direction at once — not pick one and optimise for it. The industry hasn't solved this. Regulators haven't caught up. "Don't worry, their Compliance stack looks like it was built in C++"

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

I started my career in Financial Services with a realist view of the financial world - the goal is to make money. After the Global Financial Crisis (GFC), things changed A LOT - the pendulum swung towards regulatory controls and stricter financial scrutiny, which left people confused if the goal was to make money or to protect consumers and markets. Many financial professionals lost hope, quit the trading floor and saw compliance as a more lucrative career. More on that below.

In practice, the regulatory burden has been spiralling for a while, and Europe has been leading the pack. Since 1991, Europe has introduced six, yes 6, Anti Money Laundering Directives that aim to combat the changing game of financial crime.

Regulations have also been a money spinner for Regulators, with fines globally hitting over$55bn since the GFC, with the US being the biggest benefactor with $37bn of fines distributed, and European at $11bn. Maybe a lesson for Europe - it isn’t the number of Directives that counts.

### How do you spend $213M on Compliance

The result of this has meant firms have had to bulk up their compliance costs, particularly in KYC/AML. After Binance was recently fined $4bn in the US for AML breaches, they quickly introduced further controls taking their annual compliance costs to $213M.

How on earth you reach $213M in compliance costs may sound mind-boggling, but I’ll try to break down the two big components of this:

- People

A lot of people. So many people you forget why you bothered calling yourself a FinTech. People reviewing documents, remediating, analysing cases, writing reports…There is a concept in Compliance called 4-eyes review, this means two sets of eyes need to look at every onboarding. This broadly translates to what ever you are paying, double the cost.

- AML Compliance Software

Today the amount of data you need to analyse is unsurmountable. In the past regulators would give compliance teams a waiver for what they call a “Risk-Based” approach. This means focus your energies on higher risks and let the lower level risks solve themselves.

However, this mindset has since shifted to “If you don’t have manpower, then get software to help you.” This isn’t an ask, it is an absolute. Regulators will scrutinise your compliance software
title: "Don't worry, their Compliance stack looks like it was built in C++"

The Compliance Team Isn't Slowing You Down. Your Process Is. 

 

Agree & Join LinkedIn

` `` `` `` `` `` `` `

---

 

Compliance and marketing have never operated on the same timeline inside a financial institution. In a stable rate environment, that friction was manageable. In the environment banks are navigating now, it is not. This edition of Naehas Navigator is about what the institutions that resolved it did first. 

 

### $175 Billion in Rate Cuts 

 

The Federal Reserve cut rates by a combined 175 basis points across 2024 and 2025. Banks entered 2026 facing headwinds in net interest income while competition for deposits remains intense, particularly among regional institutions. Consumers are more attentive to rate than they have been in years. The window to act is real, and it is compressed. 

 

Most institutions have the strategy. Few have the infrastructure to execute it at the speed the market now requires. Product launches still move through manual review cycles. Disclosures still require weeks of coordination. A decision gets made in a boardroom and stalls somewhere between legal, marketing, and product before it ever reaches a customer. 

 

The institutions differentiating themselves right now are not doing so on rate alone. They are the ones where a product decision made on Monday reaches a customer on Thursday. 

 

---

 

### A $215 Billion Bank Evaluated Every Major Vendor 

 

A $215 billion institution evaluated every major platform in this space before making a selection. The criteria reflected something most RFPs fail to surface: whether a platform could support the actual workflow at scale, without making engineering a dependency for routine changes. 

 

The institution had been repeatedly rebuilding the same base products. Every pricing modification and disclosure update required a separate development project. The business had a strategy. The infrastructure could not keep pace with it. 

 

Four things determined the outcome: integrated product, pricing, and disclosure management in a single environment; customization demonstrated live rather than promised on a roadmap; governance controls that preserved compliance without adding friction; and an API-first architecture aligned with how modern institutions operate.

# KYC Risk API Failure: Silent Compliance Gaps

founder & CEO

When Your KYC Risk Categorization API Fails During High-Risk Monitoring **KYC Risk API Failure: The Silent Compliance Gap** Your KYC API flags high-risk accounts, but the monitoring layer fails to escalate. This creates 72-hour audit gaps where no enhanced due diligence occurs - a regulatory nightmare. **What Failure Means** System decoupling: API correctly categorizes "high-risk," but workflows for manual review, screening, and source-of-funds checks don't trigger. Cases drop silently into default queues. **Under Audit & Volume Pressure** Categorization latency spikes cause monitoring to evaluate stale/null risk scores. Manual "batch re-runs" create un-auditable parallel processes with new compliance gaps. **Breaking Assumptions** Assuming API output is complete risk signal ignores real-time data feed dependencies (PEP lists, sanctions, adverse media). Downtime creates verification drift only discovered during audits. **Solutions** Internal fixes work only for integration issues. For monitoring logic failures (jurisdiction-specific rules), redesign decisioning layers. Multi-source verification platforms become requirements, not enhancements. **Key Issues** - API latency from synchronous external calls - Silent failures in asynchronous webhooks - Audit timestamp correlation reveals gaps - Jurisdiction-specific rule application failures **Critical Takeaway**: When categorization works but monitoring fails, you face procedural compliance failures regardless of technical causes.

The fintech and payments industry thrives on speed, from instant payments to real-time fraud detection. Yet, with increasing regulatory scrutiny and evolving financial crimes, emerging companies must also prioritize security and scalability to sustain growth. How can compliance teams ensure that effective risk management keeps pace with innovation without becoming a bottleneck? 

 

As compliance leaders, we are not just gatekeepers — We enable sustainable, secure, and scalable growth of the firms we are in. Let’s take a look to see how we can strike this delicate balance: 

 

### 1. Speed: The Competitive Edge That Compliance Must Support 

 

### The Challenge 

 

Fintechs and payment companies differentiate themselves through speed. Seamless onboarding, rapid transactions, and instant cross-border settlements are some of the key offerings that differ from what clients are used to from large financial institutions. However, compliance requirements are often seen as a barrier, with lengthy due diligence and regulatory checks, leading to delays in go-to-market strategies. 

 

### The Solution 

 

Smart automation and risk-based decisions can help compliance teams match the need for speed without compromising integrity. At Finmo , we are supporting speedy client onboarding and critical merchant activities with the following elements: 

 

- AI-Driven KYC & KYB: Implementing AI-enhanced identity verification can reduce onboarding time from days to hours, if not minutes, vastly improving customer experience while meeting regulatory standards. 
- Real-Time Transaction Monitoring: Versatile and highly configurable rules, coupled with Machine Learning (ML) models can identify suspicious activities in real-time, eliminating batch-based reviews that slow down transactions. 
- Product-Compliance Integrations: Embedding compliance controls directly into the payment workflows enables compliance at the speed of business, rather than as a roadblock.

5d Edited

I have sat in rooms where careers ended because of a compliance failure. Not fraud. Not misconduct. A gap in regulatory knowledge that nobody caught in time. A sanctions check that missed a designation. A policy that had not been updated to reflect a regulatory change that happened six months earlier. The fine was not the worst part. The worst part was watching someone who had spent 25 years building a reputation, a genuinely good professional, carry the weight of something that better information would have prevented. This is what people do not say loudly enough about compliance failures: they are rarely the result of bad intent. They are almost always the result of impossible workloads, fragmented information, and research processes that have not changed in a decade. A compliance team at a mid-sized bank is expected to monitor regulatory developments across multiple jurisdictions, maintain policy frameworks, manage audits, support the business, and stay current on enforcement trends simultaneously with lean resources. The information exists. It is just scattered across regulator websites, consultation papers, guidance notes, and legal databases that nobody has time to read in full. That gap, between the information that exists and the information that reaches the right person at the right time, is where careers end. Where fines land. Where reputations collapse. I spent 20 years watching it happen. And I became convinced that the problem was never the people. It was the tools they were given to do an impossible job. That is the problem I built Sherlocq to solve. If you are navigating this environment, Sherlocq exists to change that. Privacy-First KYC Architecture Failing Under Global Compliance Audits # Privacy-First KYC Architecture Failing Under Global Compliance Audits Privacy-first KYC systems can fail during regulatory audits, turning compliance architecture into risk sources. Verification failures cause complete workflow halts, creating backlogs that auditors flag as control gaps. ## Audit Pressure & System Failure Under pressure, teams bypass privacy layers, exposing raw PII in logs and tickets - violating principles and creating evidence trails of non-compliance. The core mistake: assuming tokenization removes dependencies. When primary government ID databases fail, privacy-first pipelines fail silently, approving nothing. ## Systemic Issues & Solutions Internal fixes often stop at source integration boundaries. Systems needing fallback logic across multiple verification sources without breaking privacy promises require redesign. Multi-source verification coordination is critical - a function specialized platforms manage. ## Key Audit Risks - \*\*Latency\*\*: First sign of privacy gateway retrying against unresponsive sources - \*\*Audit Failure\*\*: Can't trace verification to authoritative sources due to architectural obfuscation - \*\*High Volume\*\*: Queues form, timeouts increase, manual review creates fraud risk ## Solution Approach If failures stem from source dependencies \(not code\), rebuilding won't help. Need systems designed for source failure, often requiring specialized verification layer integration. \*Privacy-first KYC must balance data protection with reliable verification to survive compliance audits.\*

content:
High-Risk Compliance for BaaS and Correspondent Banks

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

- The regulator doesn't require you to ‘know the Truth,’ or ‘to not make mistakes’
- No one is 100% bad or good: there's your (lack of) focus on the customer
- No perfect KYC at onboarding will protect you from fraud
- Compliance is about “being trackable” (the ability to trace, “follow the money”)
- Starting from January 1st, FinCEN introduced a new regulation, AMLA, which requires banks to conduct KYCC, which affects correspondent and BaaS banks the most

"Simply forget about KYC; let anyone who desires a bank account have one, and use AI/ML to track the bad actors," said David Birch, and he was correct. Soups Ranjan made a similar comment to me recently about working with high-risk clients - "KYC is just the simplest part, the real interest starts afterward."

Regulators aren't focused on the "truth" - what matters to them is that you've considered these types of risks. Compliance is often wrongly seen as a mechanism to differentiate good people from bad. In reality, compliance is about "being trackable" (the ability to trace, to "follow the money") through a set of dynamic parameters over time. It's vital to realize that no perfect KYC at the onboarding stage can safeguard you against fraud and scammers: only about 20% of malefactors can be caught upon registration.

No individual is wholly bad or entirely good; broadly speaking, compliance isn't about "preventing September 11 from happening" but rather, when something does happen, it's about "quickly identifying who is involved." There's no black and white in this - it's all about focusing on the customer, understanding them, the risks they bring, and how to monitor and manage those risks effectively.

Everyone has seen films where the FBI or other special services, exhausted from chasing a hacker, eventually propose they switch sides to assist in catching other hackers and criminals. So, why don't compliance departments hire individuals convicted (or currently imprisoned) for 'money laundering'? They understand compliance and can immediately spot suspicious patterns far better than any specialist. The same applies to risk and the creation of new credit products - instead of seeking advice from pampered business school graduates, who recommend stepping out of one's comfort zone while sipping 18-year-old whiske
title: High-Risk Compliance for BaaS and Correspondent Banks

Interview with the founder of Invoid, a Y Combinator startup from the W’21 batch. Invoid streamlined and automated individual onboarding and identity verification operations. The company's platform provided back-end services such as video, digital KYC, and document verification, enabling clients to onboard and detect fraudulent information, scale faster, and cut down on operations as well as expenses.

Sarthak, one of the founders of Invoid, walked us through all the early days up to the exit phase of Invoid.

Invoid was a company that started with an initial investment of $10,000 and sustained on their revenue, essentially bootstrapping until the first round of investments from YC and other VCs. At their peak, they were making $200,000 MRR. It was an opportunity for them to exit, and they took that opportunity.

🎉Invoid was acquired by Bureau ID in 2023.🎉

Enjoy!

### Could you please introduce yourself and your startup?

My name is Sarthak. I co-founded a company called Invoid, where we help companies automate their KYC processes and create identity-based workflows in their onboarding journey.

Compliance and KYC are fundamental for many companies. Some companies find it mandatory, while others use it for fraud checks and prevention on their platforms. We served companies across these domains.

We were probably the fastest-growing in the country, being the only developer-friendly KYC product available. Other products had become incumbent, requiring more integration time and not offering a full suite of services. Our competitive advantage was that we were full-stack and end-to-end in many cases, plus we were developer-friendly, which allowed us to serve various industries. This included fintech, banking, gaming, crypto, wealth tech, dating, and social networking. In most of these domains, KYC wasn't mandatory, but our service was easy to use and comparatively budget-friendly, which helped companies reduce fraud.

We also had a couple of proprietary AI models that we developed in-house, giving us a competitive edge. That's what Invoid did. # Varun Jain’s Post

2mo

The most common thing I hear from early-stage founders? “We spend more time on compliance than we do on product.” Here's the math nobody talks about: A 15-person startup gets a SOC 2 request from a potential customer. The CTO spends 3 hours/week on compliance. The senior engineer spends 5 hours/week gathering evidence. Someone on the team is manually answering security questionnaires. That's 15+ hours/week of NOT building product. Over a quarter, that's 250 hours. Gone. And the worst part? This repeats. Every year. With every new framework. With every new security questionnaire. Early-stage companies can't afford this tax. You didn't raise money to take screenshots of your AWS configs. You raised money to build something customers love. The fix isn't "hire a compliance person." At 15 people, you can't justify that headcount. The fix is automation that connects to your actual infrastructure and does the evidence collection for you. So your engineers can go back to engineering. That's why we built ComplyJet. Because your engineers' time is too valuable to waste on screenshots.
